1:
"And Jesus entered and passed through Jericho." The witnesses note the setting of the Zacchaeus narrative, peculiar to Luke. Ambrose frames the chapter's grace: "upon the one our Lord waits to show mercy" — the blind man of the previous chapter — "upon the other He confers the great glory of abiding in his house. The chief among the publicans is here fitly introduced; for who will hereafter despair of himself, now that he attains to grace who gained his living by fraud? And he too moreover a rich man, that we may know that not all rich men are covetous." Cyril notes that Christ "calls near those who are afar off, and gives light to those who are in darkness."

The witnesses note Jericho as the place where, passing through, Christ would call the chief publican — a city of priests, yet the scene of a publican's conversion.

So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of the conversion of Zacchaeus. The lesson the expositors draw is the encouragement that the grace of Christ reaches even the most unlikely. Ambrose's reflection, the witnesses note, sets the keynote: the calling of Zacchaeus — a man who had grown rich by the fraud and extortion proverbial in his trade — is the proof that none need despair of mercy; if a chief publican can be saved, no sinner is beyond the reach of grace. The expositors press the further lesson Ambrose draws: that Zacchaeus was rich, and yet was saved, teaching "that not all rich men are covetous" — that even wealth, though a snare, is no bar to the kingdom where the heart is changed, against the despairing word that the rich can scarcely be saved. Cyril's note, the witnesses observe, marks the breadth of Christ's mercy: he calls those afar off, gives light to those in darkness — and Zacchaeus, far off in his sin and his despised office, is about to be called near. The expositors note the fitness of the setting: Jericho, on the road up to Jerusalem and the cross, becomes the scene of a salvation that displays in miniature the very errand on which Christ was bound — to seek and to save the lost. The verse thus opens the account of Zacchaeus with the quiet entrance of Christ into Jericho, an entrance that would change a chief publican's life; and it prepares the reader to wonder, with the fathers, at a grace that stoops to the worst, saves the rich, and calls the far-off near — the grace that the whole narrative will unfold, and that no sinner, beholding Zacchaeus, need ever despair of.
2:
"And, behold, there was a man named Zacchaeus, which was the chief among the publicans, and he was rich." The witnesses note the man's name, office, and wealth. The Reformed Gill establishes his identity as a Jew despite his despised trade: "there were Jews that were publicans, as Levi or Matthew... and such an one was Zacchaeus" — the name (Zaccai) being a known Jewish name. Cyril describes the office in its worst light: Zacchaeus "was chief of the publicans, a man entirely abandoned to covetousness, whose sole object was the increase of his gains; for such was the practice of the publicans, though Paul calls it idolatry" (cf. Colossians 3:5). Cyril notes the Lord "justly joined them with the harlots" (Matthew 21:31), yet "Zacchaeus continued not among their number, but was counted worthy of mercy."

The witnesses note the three marks: chief publican (the head of the tax-farmers), and rich — a station and a wealth both gained, by repute, through extortion.

So the witnesses make the verse the portrait of the man Christ would save. The lesson the expositors draw is the unlikeliness of the convert and the greatness of the grace that reached him. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, was "chief among the publicans" — not merely a tax-collector but the head of them, the most prominent and, in the popular eye, the most guilty; and he "was rich," his wealth amassed (as the publicans' was) by the extortion and fraud their office afforded. The expositors press Cyril's severe estimate: the publican's trade was reckoned akin to idolatry, abandoned to covetousness, and the chief of them the chief offender; such men were classed with harlots as the outcasts of Jewish society. Yet, the witnesses observe, this very man — rich, chief publican, abandoned (as men thought) to greed — was about to be "counted worthy of mercy at Christ's hands." The expositors draw the lesson: grace chooses the unlikely, and saves where men would least expect; the head of the publicans, the rich extortioner, the despised outcast, is the one on whom Christ sets his eye. The witnesses note Gill's care to establish Zacchaeus as a Jew — a son of Abraham (as Christ will call him) — yet one who, by his office, had cut himself off from his people and seemed lost. The verse thus introduces the man in all his apparent disqualification — chief publican, rich, abandoned to gain — that the grace which seeks and saves the lost might be magnified the more; for the worse the man, the brighter the mercy that calls him, and Zacchaeus, the chief of sinners in his city, would become the trophy of the Saviour who came to seek and to save the lost.
3:
"And he sought to see Jesus who he was; and could not for the press, because he was little of stature." The witnesses note Zacchaeus's earnest desire and the obstacles to it. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads the desire as natural curiosity overruled to good: "he had that curiosity which is natural to men to see one of whom they have heard much; and it would seem that in this case mere curiosity led to his conversion... God makes use of every principle — of curiosity, sympathy, affection, hope, or fear — to lead men in the way of salvation." Titus of Bostra reads it as the dawning of grace: "the seed of salvation had begun to spring up in him, for he desired to see Jesus, having never seen Him; for no one that sees Jesus can remain any longer in wickedness." Titus names two obstacles: "the multitude, not so much of men as of his sins, prevented him, for he was little of stature."

The witnesses note the double hindrance: the crowd that pressed about Christ, and Zacchaeus's own short stature, which kept him from seeing over them.

So the witnesses make the verse the picture of an earnest seeker hindered. The lesson the expositors draw is that God overrules even natural curiosity to the saving of the soul, and that earnest desire surmounts every obstacle. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, "sought to see Jesus who he was" — at first, Barnes judges, from no higher motive than the common curiosity to see a famous man; yet this curiosity was the seed God would water into faith. The expositors press the comforting truth: God uses every principle of the human heart — curiosity, sympathy, hope, fear — to draw men toward salvation; a small and natural desire may be the beginning of a great work of grace, as it was in Zacchaeus. Titus's reading, the witnesses observe, sees in the very desire the dawning of grace — the seed of salvation springing up, for none who truly sees Jesus can remain in his wickedness. And the obstacles, the expositors note, are instructive: the "press," the crowd, hindered him — and Titus reads it spiritually, that it was not the multitude of men only but the multitude of his sins that stood between him and Christ; and his "little stature" kept him from seeing over the throng. The witnesses draw the lesson: the earnest seeker is not stopped by hindrances but, as the next verse shows, finds a way past them; Zacchaeus, hindered by crowd and stature, does not give up but runs ahead and climbs. The verse thus sets the seeking publican against the obstacles to his seeing Christ — the crowd, his sins, his stature — and prepares for the resourceful eagerness by which he overcame them; teaching that where there is true desire after Christ, no obstacle, outward or inward, will be allowed to keep the soul from him.
4:
"And he ran before, and climbed up into a sycomore tree to see him: for he was to pass that way." The witnesses note Zacchaeus's resourceful eagerness. Adam Clarke draws the lesson from his short stature turned to advantage: "the shortness of his stature was amply compensated by his agility and invention. Had he been as tall as the generality of the crowd, he might have been equally unnoticed; his getting into the tree made him conspicuous... Even the imperfections of our persons may become subservient to the grace of God in our eternal salvation." Ellicott identifies the tree as the fig-mulberry (Ficus sycomorus), which "grew to a considerable height in the Jordan valley." The witnesses note Zacchaeus, undeterred by crowd and stature, ran ahead of the company and climbed a tree to see Christ pass.

The witnesses note the lengths he went to: a man of wealth and office, running like a boy and climbing a tree, that he might see Jesus.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof of Zacchaeus's earnest determination. The lesson the expositors draw is that true desire after Christ will overcome every obstacle and stoop to any means. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, hindered by the crowd and his short stature, did not abandon his purpose but found a way: he "ran before" the company, outpacing them to a point on the road, and "climbed up into a sycomore tree" — a wealthy chief publican, careless of his dignity, running and climbing like a child, that he might catch sight of Jesus. The expositors press the lesson Clarke draws: the very imperfection that hindered him — his shortness — became, by his resourcefulness, the occasion of his being seen; for had he been tall, he would have stood unnoticed in the crowd, but his climbing made him conspicuous, and so drew the eye and the word of Christ. Thus, the witnesses observe, even the defects of our persons and circumstances may be overruled to our salvation, God using what seems a disadvantage to single us out for grace. The expositors note the spirit the verse commends: Zacchaeus would let nothing — not his dignity, his wealth, his office, the crowd, or his stature — stand between him and the sight of Christ; and this earnest, self-forgetting eagerness is the very temper that finds him. The witnesses draw the application: the soul that truly seeks Christ will count no effort too great, no means too lowly, but will run, climb, and stoop to whatever is needed to come to him. The verse thus shows the seeking publican overcoming his obstacles by resourceful determination — running and climbing for a mere sight of Jesus — and prepares for the gracious surprise of the next verse, where the One he sought to see calls him by name and asks to abide in his house.
5:
"And when Jesus came to the place, he looked up, and saw him, and said unto him, Zacchaeus, make haste, and come down; for to day I must abide at thy house." The witnesses note Christ's gracious initiative and his knowledge of the man. Cyril marks the divine sight: "Christ saw this with the eyes of Deity," and called him by name though he had never met him — a token of his omniscience and his electing grace. The witnesses note the tenderness and authority of the call: Christ takes the initiative, names Zacchaeus, bids him hasten down, and announces that he "must" lodge in his house that very day. The "must," the expositors observe, marks a divine necessity — the appointed purpose of grace that drew Christ to this house.

The witnesses note the wonder: the One Zacchaeus sought merely to see now seeks him, calls him by name, and invites himself to be his guest.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious calling of Zacchaeus by Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the free, anticipating initiative of Christ's grace, which seeks the sinner before the sinner can reach him. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, had sought only to see Jesus from a distance, hidden in a tree; but Christ "looked up, and saw him" — knew him, named him ("Zacchaeus"), though they had never met, by the eyes of Deity that read the heart — and called him down to be his host. The expositors press the wonder of the reversal: the seeker becomes the sought; the man who climbed for a glimpse is singled out, named, and honoured with the presence of Christ in his house. Cyril's note, the witnesses observe, marks the omniscience and the electing love in the call: Christ saw Zacchaeus with divine sight, knew his name and his heart, and chose his house — the grace running ahead of the man's desire and exceeding it beyond all hope. The "I must abide at thy house," the expositors note, breathes both authority and necessity: Christ, who came to seek and save the lost, was under a holy constraint to lodge with this lost one; his coming to Zacchaeus's house was no accident but the appointed errand of grace. The witnesses draw the lesson for every sinner: it is Christ who first seeks, names, and calls; the soul that stirs to seek him finds itself already sought, the desire to see him answered by his desire to dwell with us. The verse thus turns the account on its hinge — the curious seeker found, named, and called by the Saviour who must abide with him — and prepares for the joyful welcome and the salvation that would come that day to Zacchaeus's house.
6:
"And he made haste, and came down, and received him joyfully." The witnesses note the eager, joyful response of Zacchaeus. The Reformed Gill reads the power in the call: "such power went along with the words of Christ, that they immediately reached his heart, awakened his conscience, affected his mind, and drew his soul to Christ... So souls sensible of their dangerous state by nature, and apprehensive of safety in Christ, and filled with love to him, flee with all haste to him for refuge." Adam Clarke draws the lament for those who do not so respond: "how often does Christ make the proposal of lodging, not only in our house, but in our heart, without its being accepted! We lose much because we do not attend to the visitations of Christ."

The witnesses note the immediacy and gladness: Zacchaeus made haste, came down, and "received him joyfully" — no hesitation, no shame, but eager welcome.

So the witnesses make the verse the joyful reception of Christ by the converted publican. The lesson the expositors draw is the readiness and gladness with which a heart touched by grace welcomes Christ. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, obeyed the call at once: he "made haste, and came down" — answering the summons without delay, as the call had bidden him hasten — "and received him joyfully," opening his house and his heart with gladness. Gill's reading, the expositors press, finds the secret in the power of Christ's word: the call did not merely reach Zacchaeus's ears but his heart, awakening his conscience, drawing his soul, kindling love — so that his haste and joy were the fruit of grace already at work within him. The witnesses observe the spiritual pattern Gill draws: the soul made sensible of its danger and of the refuge in Christ flees to him with all haste and welcomes him with joy; for where Christ is truly received, he is received gladly, not grudgingly. Clarke's lament, the expositors note, sets the foil: how often Christ proposes to lodge not only in the house but in the heart, and is refused; how much men lose by not attending to his visitations. Zacchaeus, by contrast, did not let the moment pass, but received the visiting Christ with open joy. The witnesses draw the lesson: the proof of a heart changed by grace is the glad and immediate reception of Christ — to welcome him without delay, without shame before the murmuring crowd, with the joy of one who has found his refuge and his Lord. The verse thus shows the seeking publican now the joyful host of Christ, his haste and gladness the first fruits of the salvation that had come to his house; and it rebukes, in Clarke's words, all who let the visiting Saviour pass their door unwelcomed.
7:
"And when they saw it, they all murmured, saying, That he was gone to be guest with a man that is a sinner." The witnesses note the murmuring of the crowd at Christ's choice of host. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the offence: "all publicans they regarded as great sinners, and the chief of the publicans therefore they regarded as peculiarly wicked... But the people seemed to forget that he might be a penitent, and that the Messiah came to save that which was lost." Ellicott notes the continuous tense — "were all murmuring." The witnesses note the same complaint that had met Christ at the call of Matthew and the reception of sinners (Luke 15:2): that he would lodge with one accounted a notorious sinner.

The witnesses note the universality of the murmur — "they all murmured" — at the sight of Christ entering a publican's house.

So the witnesses make the verse the self-righteous complaint against Christ's grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the murmuring of the self-righteous and the saving purpose of Christ. The crowd, the witnesses note, "all murmured" when they saw Christ go to lodge with Zacchaeus — for the chief publican was, in their eyes, the very chief of sinners, a man of notorious wickedness, with whom a holy teacher should have no dealings. The expositors press the root of the offence Barnes names: they reckoned all publicans great sinners, and the chief publican peculiarly so; and they could not conceive that Christ went to him to save, not to sanction his sin. The witnesses observe the recurring pattern: this is the same complaint that had dogged Christ throughout — "this man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them" (Luke 15:2) — the murmur of those who despised the lost and grudged them the Saviour. The expositors note what the murmurers forgot: that Zacchaeus might be a penitent, and that the very errand of the Messiah was "to seek and to save that which was lost" (as Christ will presently declare); their self-righteousness blinded them to the possibility of grace, so that they saw only a sinner's house, not a soul being saved. The witnesses draw the lesson: the grace of Christ toward the worst of sinners is ever a scandal to the self-righteous, who would have him keep company only with the respectable; but Christ goes where the need is greatest, lodging with the publican that he might save him. The verse thus sets the crowd's murmur against Christ's mercy, exposing the loveless spirit that grudges the lost their Saviour; and it prepares for Zacchaeus's confession and Christ's vindication, which would prove the murmurers wrong — for salvation had come that day to the very house they despised.
8:
"And Zacchaeus stood, and said unto the Lord; Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor; and if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold." The witnesses note Zacchaeus's confession and restitution as the fruit of his conversion. Lightfoot sets it against the Jewish rules: alms from honest wealth were not required beyond "a fifth part," yet Zacchaeus gives half; and restitution of ill-gotten gain was required — his "fourfold" matching the law's penalty for theft (Exodus 22:1). The Reformed Gill notes he "stood" before Christ, "to make a public confession, that they might all hear it." The witnesses note the two acts: liberal giving of half his goods to the poor, and fourfold restitution of whatever he had extorted.

The witnesses note the change wrought in him: the covetous extortioner now gives half his wealth away and restores fourfold all he had wrongfully taken.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof of Zacchaeus's genuine conversion. The lesson the expositors draw is that true repentance bears fruit in liberality and restitution. Zacchaeus, the witnesses note, "stood" before Christ and the crowd and made open confession — not in words of contrition only, but in deeds that proved his heart was changed. The two acts, the expositors press, are the marks of a real turning. First, liberality: "the half of my goods I give to the poor" — the covetous man, whose sole object had been gain, now gives away half his wealth, far exceeding (as Lightfoot notes) the fifth that Jewish custom required; the love of money that had ruled him is broken, and charity has taken its place. Second, restitution: "if I have taken any thing by false accusation, I restore him fourfold" — owning the extortion of his trade, he makes amends not merely by returning what he took, but by the fourfold restitution the law imposed on a convicted thief (Exodus 22:1), going beyond bare justice to the fullest reparation. The witnesses observe the genuineness this proves: repentance that costs nothing is no repentance; but Zacchaeus's turning struck at the very sin that had defined him — his covetousness and his fraud — and made restitution where he had wronged. The expositors draw the lesson: the converted heart does not merely profess sorrow but forsakes its sin and repairs its wrongs; faith that is real shows itself in works of justice and mercy. The witnesses note the rebuke this offers the murmuring crowd: the man they called a sinner proved, by his fruits, more righteous than they; his confession answered their complaint. The verse thus displays the fruit of grace in Zacchaeus — liberality to the poor and fourfold restitution to the wronged — the unmistakable evidence that salvation had indeed come to his house.
9:
"And Jesus said unto him, This day is salvation come to this house, forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham." The witnesses note Christ's declaration of salvation and the meaning of "son of Abraham." The Reformed Gill and Lightfoot weigh the phrase: most take "son of Abraham" as a son by faith — "which indeed is most true" — though Lightfoot suggests it may also assert Zacchaeus's natural descent from Abraham, against those who, despising the publican, would scarcely own him a Jew at all. The witnesses note Christ pronounces "salvation come to this house" — the house the murmurers despised — that very day, the day of Zacchaeus's conversion.

The witnesses note the public vindication: Christ declares that salvation has come, and claims Zacchaeus as a true son of Abraham, against the crowd that counted him a mere sinner.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's declaration of Zacchaeus's salvation and his answer to the murmurers. The lesson the expositors draw is that salvation comes to the penitent believer, who is thereby proved a true child of Abraham, whatever his past or his station. Christ, the witnesses note, having heard Zacchaeus's confession, pronounces the verdict: "This day is salvation come to this house" — salvation, that very day, to the publican and his household; the murmurers had said Christ went to a sinner's house, and Christ answers that salvation had come to it. The expositors press the fulness of the word: not merely pardon, but salvation — the whole blessing of grace, deliverance, and life — had entered with Christ and been received by faith. The ground Christ gives, the witnesses observe, is "forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham." The expositors weigh the phrase: most rightly take it of spiritual sonship — Zacchaeus, by his faith and repentance, proved himself a true child of Abraham, who was justified by faith, and so an heir of the promise; the publican, despised as an outcast, was in truth a son of the father of the faithful. Lightfoot's note, the witnesses observe, adds that the phrase also reclaims Zacchaeus's natural standing: the crowd had all but unchurched him for his trade, but Christ owns him a son of Abraham still, both by descent and by faith. The expositors draw the lesson against the murmurers: the man they would exclude, Christ includes; the sinner they despised, Christ declares saved and a true son of the covenant. The verse thus crowns the conversion of Zacchaeus with Christ's own word of salvation, and vindicates the grace that the self-righteous grudged: salvation had come to that house, and the chief publican was a son of Abraham — saved by the faith that works, and owned by the Saviour who came to seek and save the lost.
10:
"For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost." The witnesses note the great summary of Christ's mission, the keynote of the whole narrative. Ellicott marks the advance over a like saying: where before (Matthew 18:11) the loving purpose rested on the "little child," here it rests on the publican "whose manhood had been marred" by the world's offences — "there it had been that He came to save; here it is that He came to seek as well." Robertson notes "the lost" (a perfect participle, the collective whole) and refers to the lost of Luke 15 — the sheep, the coin, the son. The witnesses note Christ gives the reason for all that has happened: he saved Zacchaeus because seeking and saving the lost is the very purpose of his coming.

The witnesses note the two verbs: Christ came not only "to save" but "to seek" — to go after the lost, as he sought out Zacchaeus.

So the witnesses make the verse the crowning declaration of Christ's saving errand. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seeking and saving of the lost is the very purpose of the Son of man's coming, exemplified in Zacchaeus. Christ, the witnesses note, answers the murmurers' complaint and grounds the salvation of Zacchaeus in one great word: "the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost." This, the expositors press, is the summary of his whole mission and the justification of all he had done — he went to the publican's house, not in despite of his being a sinner, but because his being lost was the very reason for the visit; for to seek and save such is why the Son of man came. The witnesses observe Ellicott's careful note on the two verbs: it is not merely that Christ came "to save" the lost when they come to him, but that he came "to seek" them — to go after the wandering, as the shepherd sought the sheep and the woman the coin (Luke 15); Zacchaeus had sought to see Jesus, but it was Jesus who truly sought and found Zacchaeus. The expositors draw the breadth of the comfort: none is too lost to be sought; the Son of man's errand reaches to the chief of sinners, the marred and ruined, the outcast publican — for he came for the very purpose of seeking and saving such. The witnesses note the rebuke to the self-righteous: they murmured that Christ went to a sinner, not knowing that the seeking of sinners was his whole work; their complaint condemned the Saviour for doing the very thing he came to do. The verse thus closes the account of Zacchaeus with the golden text of the gospel — that the Son of man came to seek and to save the lost — making the conversion of one chief publican the living illustration of the purpose for which Christ came into the world; a word of hope to every lost soul, and a rebuke to all who would grudge the lost their seeking, saving Lord.
11:
"And as they heard these things, he added and spake a parable, because he was nigh to Jerusalem, and because they thought that the kingdom of God should immediately appear." The witnesses note the occasion of the parable of the pounds. Eusebius explains: "there were some who thought that our Saviour's kingdom would commence at His first coming, and they were expecting it shortly to appear when He was preparing to go up to Jerusalem; so astonished were they by the divine miracles." Christ therefore "informs them that He should not receive the kingdom from His Father until He had left mankind to go to His Father." Theophylact reads the parable's frame: "by His setting out into a far country, He denotes His own ascension; when He adds, to receive a kingdom and to return, He points out His second appearance, when He shall come as a King in great glory."

The witnesses note the false expectation Christ corrects: that the kingdom of God should "immediately appear" as he neared Jerusalem.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction to the parable of the pounds. The lesson the expositors draw is the correction of the carnal hope that the kingdom would come at once, and the framing of the long interval before Christ's return. The disciples and the crowd, the witnesses note, expecting (like the Pharisees of chapter 17) a visible, immediate kingdom, supposed that as Christ drew near to Jerusalem he would there and then set up his reign in glory. Christ corrects this, the expositors press, by a parable whose very structure teaches the truth: a nobleman goes "into a far country to receive a kingdom, and to return" — and the going and the return mark the two comings, with a long interval between, during which the servants must trade with their lord's money. Theophylact's reading, the witnesses observe, unfolds the figure: the going into the far country is Christ's ascension to the Father, where he receives the kingdom; the return is his second coming in glory; and between the two lies the present age of service and stewardship, in which his servants are tried. The expositors note the wisdom of the correction: the kingdom would not "immediately appear" in the outward glory they craved, but Christ must first ascend, leave his servants with their trust, and only at his return reward the faithful and judge the rebellious. The witnesses draw the lesson: the disciples must not look for the kingdom's outward triumph at once, but settle to the long work of faithful stewardship in their Lord's absence, awaiting his return. The verse thus sets the parable as a corrective to false eschatological haste, and frames the whole as a teaching on the interval between Christ's departure and his coming — the time of trading with the pounds, in which every servant's faithfulness is proved.
12:
"He said therefore, A certain nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return." The witnesses note the figure of the nobleman and its historical and spiritual grounding. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the historical background: "this expression is derived from the state of things in Judea... it was necessary that the prince should receive a recognition of his right to the kingdom by the Roman emperor, and in order to this, that he should go to Rome" — as Archelaus, son of Herod, "went to Rome to obtain confirmation of the title his father left him." Theophylact gives the spiritual sense: Christ "calls Himself a man, because of His nativity in the flesh; then noble; not yet a King, because at His first appearance He exercised no kingly power; and 'to obtain a kingdom,' according to Daniel, Behold one like the Son of man" (Daniel 7:13-14).

The witnesses note the figure: a noble who must journey to a distant court to receive royal authority, and then return to reign — the pattern of Christ's ascension and coming.

So the witnesses make the verse the central figure of the parable. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, the noble Son of man, ascends to receive his kingdom and will return to reign. The historical groundwork, the witnesses note, would have been vivid to Christ's hearers: just as the Herodian princes had to journey to Rome to have their titles confirmed by the emperor before they could reign in Judea, so the "nobleman" of the parable goes "into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return." The expositors press the spiritual application Theophylact draws: Christ is the "man" by his incarnation, the "noble" by his divine dignity and royal lineage, yet "not yet a King" in outward power at his first coming; he goes into the far country — ascends to the Father — to receive the kingdom (as Daniel saw the Son of man brought before the Ancient of Days to receive dominion, Daniel 7:13-14); and he will "return" in glory to reign. The witnesses observe the answer this gives to the false expectation of verse 11: the kingdom would not appear immediately, for the King must first depart to receive it, and only then return. The expositors note the comfort and the summons in the figure: Christ's present absence is not defeat but the journey to receive his crown; his servants are to occupy until he comes; and his return is certain, to reward and to reign. The witnesses draw the lesson: the Church lives in the interval of the nobleman's absence, between the receiving of the kingdom and the return to reign — a time of stewardship and trial, awaiting the King who will surely come back in power. The verse thus sets the figure of the departing and returning noble at the heart of the parable, teaching the ascension, the heavenly enthronement, and the certain return of Christ to reign over a kingdom received from the Father.
13:
"And he called his ten servants, and delivered them ten pounds, and said unto them, Occupy till I come." The witnesses note the servants, the pounds, and the charge to trade. The Reformed Gill identifies the servants chiefly as "the ministers of the Gospel," the call being "to the office and work of the ministry"; the "ten" he takes as "a round number, for an uncertain one." Lightfoot notes the parable of the pounds "hath the very same scope with that of the talents" (Matthew 25), yet (as the moderns add) is distinct: here each servant receives the same single pound. The "occupy till I come," the witnesses note, is the charge to trade with the entrusted money — to put it to use and increase it — until the lord's return.

The witnesses note the equal entrustment: each of the ten receives one pound, with the same command to occupy until the lord comes.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrusting of the pounds and the charge of stewardship. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ commits to his servants a trust to be used in his absence, with the charge to be diligent until he comes. The "ten servants," the witnesses note, represent the servants of Christ — chiefly, Gill judges, the ministers of the gospel, though by extension all to whom Christ entrusts gifts and opportunities; and the "ten pounds," one to each, are the trust committed to them. The expositors note the distinctive feature, which Clarke (on the next verses) draws out: each servant receives the same single pound, where in the parable of the talents the sums differed — the pound here figuring, many hold, the gift of the gospel, which is the same to all who receive it, while the talents figure the differing capacities by which it is improved. The charge, the witnesses press, is "Occupy till I come" — that is, trade, employ, put to use the entrusted pound, that it may gain increase; the servant is not to hoard or hide the trust but to make it fruitful in his lord's service. The expositors observe the abiding lesson: Christ, ascending to receive his kingdom, leaves his servants with a trust — gifts, the gospel, opportunities for service — and commands them to be busy and diligent with it during the long interval of his absence, until his return. The witnesses note the emphasis of "till I come": the stewardship is for the whole interval, the servant ever to be occupied, never idle, awaiting the account. The verse thus lays the foundation of the parable's lesson: the servants are entrusted, charged, and left to trade; and their faithfulness or sloth in occupying the pound till the lord comes will be the ground of their reward or doom when he returns.
14:
"But his citizens hated him, and sent a message after him, saying, We will not have this man to reign over us." The witnesses note the rebellious citizens and their meaning. The Reformed Gill identifies them as "the Jews, who were his own people and nation," who "hated him with a mortal hatred, as appeared by their traducing his person, vilifying his doctrine, ascribing his miracles to a diabolical influence, and persecuting his disciples." Barnes notes the historical ground again: this "was, in fact, the case with regard to Archelaus," whose discontented subjects sent an embassy to Rome to oppose his confirmation. The witnesses note the open rebellion: not merely indifference, but a declared refusal — "We will not have this man to reign over us."

The witnesses note the citizens, distinct from the servants, who openly reject the nobleman's rule and send their protest after him.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejection of Christ's reign by his own people. The lesson the expositors draw is the open rebellion of those who refuse the King, and the certain judgment it invites. The "citizens," the witnesses note, are distinguished from the servants: while the servants receive the trust, the citizens are the subjects who hate their lord and reject his rule. Gill reads them, the expositors press, as the Jewish nation — Christ's own people, to whom he came and over whom he had every right to reign — who "hated him with a mortal hatred," and proved it by reviling his person, slandering his doctrine, attributing his miracles to Satan, and persecuting his followers; their cry "We will not have this man to reign over us" was uttered in deed when they crucified the King of the Jews and rejected his gospel. The witnesses observe the historical aptness Barnes notes: as the subjects of Archelaus sent an embassy to Rome to oppose his kingship, so the Jews sent their rejection after the ascending Christ, refusing his reign. The expositors draw the broader application: the citizens figure all who refuse Christ's rule — who will not have him to reign over them, but cast off his authority and reject his crown. The witnesses note the gravity of such rebellion: it is not passive neglect but active enmity, a declared refusal of the King; and (as verse 27 will show) it ends in fearful judgment, the rebels slain before the returned King. The verse thus introduces, alongside the servants to be rewarded, the rebellious citizens to be judged — the Jewish nation that rejected its Messiah, and all who will not own Christ's reign — preparing for the double issue of the parable: reward for the faithful servants, and destruction for the enemies who refused the King.
15:
"And it came to pass, that when he was returned, having received the kingdom, then he commanded these servants to be called unto him, to whom he had given the money, that he might know how much every man had gained by trading." The witnesses note the return of the king and the reckoning. The Reformed Gill reads the return of two senses: "in power to Jerusalem, in the destruction of that city; or in person to this earth, at the end of the world... local and visible, and in great glory." Clarke agrees: "when he came to punish the disobedient Jews, and when he shall come to judge the world." The witnesses note the purpose of the calling: that the king "might know how much every man had gained by trading" — the account of each servant's stewardship.

The witnesses note the king, now invested with the kingdom, returns and summons his servants to give account of their trading.

So the witnesses make the verse the return of the king and the calling to account. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of a reckoning, when Christ, having received his kingdom, will return to call his servants to account for their stewardship. The king, the witnesses note, "having received the kingdom" in the far country, now "returns" — and the expositors observe the double reference Gill and Clarke draw: the return figures both the coming of Christ in judgment upon Jerusalem (AD 70), when he punished the rebellious nation, and his final coming at the end of the world, in person, visible, and in glory, to judge all men. At his return, the witnesses press, the first act toward his servants is to call them to account: "that he might know how much every man had gained by trading" — each must render up the account of his stewardship, what he has made of the pound entrusted to him. The expositors note the solemnity of this reckoning: the servants of Christ are not left to trade unwatched and unjudged, but must one day stand before the returned King and answer for their use of his gifts; the day of grace is followed by the day of account. The witnesses draw the lesson: Christ's present absence is not the end of the matter, but the interval before the reckoning; every servant trades now in view of the account he must give when the Lord comes. The expositors observe the comfort and the warning together: the faithful will be called to reward, the unfaithful to loss; but all will be called, and all will give account. The verse thus turns the parable from the entrusting to the reckoning: the king returns invested with his kingdom, and summons his servants to declare their gains — the picture of Christ's return to call his Church to account for the stewardship of his gifts; and it prepares for the rewarding of the faithful and the exposure of the slothful that follow.
16:
"Then came the first, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained ten pounds." The witnesses note the faithful servant's account and the distinctive teaching of the pounds. The Reformed Gill takes the first as among "the apostles of Christ... such an one was the Apostle Paul," who "laboured more abundantly." Adam Clarke draws the key distinction between this parable and the talents: "the mina given to each seems to point out the gift of the Gospel, which is the same to all who hear it; but the talents distributed in different proportions... intimate that God has given different capacities and advantages to men, by which this one gift may be differently improved." The witnesses note the servant ascribes the increase to the pound — "thy pound hath gained" — not to himself.

The witnesses note the great increase: the one pound has gained ten, a tenfold profit from the entrusted trust.

So the witnesses make the verse the account of the most faithful servant. The lesson the expositors draw is the rich increase that diligent stewardship of Christ's gifts produces, and the humility that ascribes it to the trust, not the self. The first servant, the witnesses note, comes with a great gain: "thy pound hath gained ten pounds" — a tenfold increase from the single pound entrusted; his diligent trading in his lord's absence has multiplied the trust tenfold. The expositors press Clarke's important distinction, the witnesses observe: here each servant received the same single pound, where in the talents the sums differed — and the difference is instructive. The pound, equal to all, figures the gospel, which is the same gift offered to every hearer; the differing returns (ten, five) figure the differing measures of faithfulness and fruitfulness with which the same gospel is improved by different servants. So the lesson, the expositors note, is not (as in the talents) of differing capacities, but of differing diligence with an equal trust: all the ministers, all the servants, receive the same gospel, and the reward answers to how faithfully each has traded with it. The witnesses observe the servant's humility: he says "thy pound hath gained ten" — ascribing the increase not to his own ability but to the pound itself, the gift entrusted; the faithful servant takes no credit for the gain, but owns it as the fruit of his lord's trust. The expositors draw the lesson: the servant of Christ who diligently uses the gospel and the gifts committed to him will see great increase, souls won and fruit borne; yet he is to ascribe all to the grace that gave the trust, not to himself. The verse thus presents the model servant — diligent, fruitful, and humble — whose tenfold gain is the pattern of faithful stewardship, and prepares for the rich reward the king is about to bestow.
17:
"And he said unto him, Well, thou good servant: because thou hast been faithful in a very little, have thou authority over ten cities." The witnesses note the commendation and the reward. The Reformed Gill expounds the "good servant" as one who, "having the grace of God which made him a good man, and gifts and abilities which made him a good minister, made a good use of them." Barnes guards the figure: "we are not to suppose that this will be literally fulfilled in heaven; Christ teaches that our reward in heaven will be in proportion to our faithfulness in improving our talents on earth." Clarke notes the reward fits the kingdom received — the faithful servant made governor over cities. The witnesses note the principle: "faithful in a very little" rewarded with great authority — small faithfulness crowned with large trust.

The witnesses note the disproportion of grace: faithfulness in a "very little" (one pound) rewarded with rule over ten cities.

So the witnesses make the verse the rewarding of the faithful servant. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ rewards faithfulness in small things with great honour, the reward far exceeding the service. The king's word, the witnesses note, is first commendation — "Well, thou good servant" — the approval of the Master, which is itself the highest reward; and Gill marks what made the servant good: the grace that made him a good man and the gifts that made him a good minister, both faithfully used. Then comes the reward, the expositors press: "because thou hast been faithful in a very little, have thou authority over ten cities." The disproportion is striking, the witnesses observe: the service was small — the trading of a single pound — yet the reward is great, authority over ten cities; and the principle is that Christ rewards faithfulness in little with dominion in much. Barnes's caution, the expositors note, guards the figure from a wooden literalism: the "ten cities" are not literal earthly governments in heaven, but figure the great honour and the enlarged sphere of blessedness that will reward faithful service — the reward proportioned to the faithfulness shown on earth. The witnesses draw the lesson the king names: "faithful in a very little" — for all our service here, however great it seems, is "a very little" beside the reward; and yet Christ counts and crowns it, exalting the faithful servant to honour and authority far beyond his deserving. The expositors observe the encouragement: no faithfulness, however small, is overlooked or unrewarded by Christ; the diligent steward of even a little will be set over much. The verse thus crowns the faithful servant with commendation and reward — "Well done" and rule over ten cities — teaching that Christ honours faithfulness in little with great glory, and that the day of account will be, for his faithful servants, a day of rich and disproportionate reward.
18:
"And the second came, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained five pounds." The witnesses note the second servant's lesser but real increase. The Reformed Gill reads him as one "who was next, though less useful, yet equally diligent and active." The witnesses note the same humble form of account — "thy pound hath gained" — and a gain of five, less than the first's ten, yet a true and substantial increase from the same single pound.

The witnesses note the second servant, faithful like the first but with a lesser return — five pounds gained, not ten.

So the witnesses make the verse the account of the second faithful servant. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ owns and rewards true faithfulness even where the fruit is less, recognizing degrees of service among his diligent servants. The second servant, the witnesses note, comes with a real but smaller gain: "thy pound hath gained five pounds" — half the increase of the first, yet a genuine and substantial fruit of faithful trading. Gill's reading, the expositors press, sets him among the diligent though less fruitful servants — "less useful, yet equally diligent and active"; for among Christ's faithful servants there are degrees, some labouring more abundantly and bearing more fruit, others faithful in their measure with a lesser return. The witnesses observe the same humble form of his account as the first: "thy pound hath gained" — ascribing the increase to the entrusted trust, not to himself; the faithful servant, whether his fruit be great or small, owns it as the gift of his lord. The expositors note the encouragement to those of lesser gifts or smaller success: the second servant is not despised for gaining only five where the first gained ten; his faithfulness is real, his gain is owned, and (as the next verse shows) he too is rewarded, in proportion to his service. The witnesses draw the lesson: Christ does not measure all his servants by the highest, nor reject the faithful whose fruit is less, but recognizes and rewards each according to his diligence and increase; the servant who gains five is faithful no less than he who gains ten, and shall have his reward. The verse thus presents the second faithful servant, whose lesser but real gain teaches that there are degrees of fruitfulness among the diligent, and that Christ owns and will reward the true faithfulness of each — preparing for the proportioned reward of the next verse, where the gain of five is crowned with authority over five cities.
19:
"And he said likewise to him, Be thou also over five cities." The witnesses note the proportioned reward of the second servant. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the present imperative — "keep on becoming over" — and presses the distinctness of this parable: "there is no real reason for identifying this parable of the pounds with the parable of the talents in Matthew 25; the versatility of Jesus needs to be remembered by those who seek to flatten out everything." The witnesses note the reward answers the gain: as the first who gained ten received ten cities, so the second who gained five receives five — the reward exactly proportioned to the fruit.

The witnesses note the precise proportion: five pounds gained, five cities given — the reward measured to the service.

So the witnesses make the verse the proportioned reward of the second servant. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ rewards his servants in exact proportion to their faithfulness and fruit. The second servant, the witnesses note, who gained five pounds, is set "over five cities" — where the first, who gained ten, received ten; the reward answers precisely to the increase. The expositors press the principle this establishes: the rewards of Christ's kingdom are not arbitrary nor uniform, but measured to the faithfulness and fruitfulness of each servant; he who has done more receives more, he who has done less receives less, yet each is rewarded according to his service. This, the witnesses observe, both honours the greater diligence of the more fruitful and yet does not despise the lesser service of the faithful: the second servant, though he gained but half what the first did, is still rewarded, and rewarded in just proportion — five cities for five pounds. Robertson's caution, the expositors note, is worth heeding: this parable is distinct from that of the talents, and not to be flattened into it; here the equal pound and the proportioned reward teach their own lesson — that with the same gospel entrusted to all, the reward answers to the diligence of each. The witnesses draw the comfort and the spur: every servant's faithfulness will be exactly weighed and exactly rewarded, none over-rewarded and none cheated; the diligent will receive according to their fruit, and the more they have gained for their Lord, the greater their reward. The verse thus completes the rewarding of the faithful with its principle of proportion — five cities for five pounds, ten for ten — teaching that Christ's rewards are measured to faithfulness, and that the servant who would have much should be much occupied with his Lord's trust; for the day of account will render to each according to his work.
20:
"And another came, saying, Lord, behold, here is thy pound, which I have kept laid up in a napkin." The witnesses note the unprofitable servant and his excuse. The grammatical-historical Barnes exposes the self-justification hidden in his words: "a napkin — he means that he had not wasted it nor thrown it by carelessly, but had been very careful of it... So many men employ their talents, their learning, their property, their influence — they have them, they keep them, but they never use them in the service of the Lord Jesus." Ellicott renders "which I kept — i.e., all along; he had never made any effort at doing more." The witnesses note the servant returns the bare pound, unincreased, wrapped and hidden — the picture of a gift received but never used.

The witnesses note the third servant brings back only the original pound, "kept laid up in a napkin" — preserved but never employed.

So the witnesses make the verse the account of the slothful, unprofitable servant. The lesson the expositors draw is the guilt of those who receive Christ's gifts but never use them in his service. The third servant, the witnesses note, comes not with gain but with the bare pound: "here is thy pound, which I have kept laid up in a napkin" — he had not lost it, not wasted it, but neither had he used it; he returns exactly what he received, no more. The expositors press the self-justifying spirit in his words, which Barnes exposes: he speaks as if his careful keeping were a virtue — he had wrapped the pound safely, not thrown it away — when in truth his very care was his condemnation, for the pound was given to be traded, not hoarded. The witnesses observe the sharp application: so many, the expositors note, have gifts — learning, property, influence, the gospel itself — and keep them, but never employ them in Christ's service; and for all the good they do the Church or the world, it would be the same had God never given them. The slothful servant, the witnesses press, figures the man who receives the trust of grace and lets it lie idle, neither denying nor using it, content to keep it safe while it bears no fruit. The expositors draw the lesson: it is not enough to receive Christ's gifts and not waste them; they must be used, traded, made fruitful in his service; the unused gift is a buried gift, and its keeper an unprofitable servant. The witnesses note the gravity the parable will assign to this sloth — the pound taken away, the servant condemned — for to receive a trust from Christ and never employ it is itself a fault deserving judgment. The verse thus introduces the unprofitable servant, whose hidden, idle pound is the emblem of every gift received from Christ but never used for him; and it prepares for the excuse he offers and the sentence he incurs.
21:
"For I feared thee, because thou art an austere man: thou takest up that thou layedst not down, and reapest that thou didst not sow." The witnesses note the servant's false excuse and his slander of his lord. The Reformed Gill marks that his fear was "not with a right fear... his fear was not of the right kind, and was ill grounded, as appears by what follows." Barnes exposes the charge: "an austere man — hard, severe, oppressive... taking every advantage, and, while he lived in idleness, making his living out of the toils of others. All this is designed to show the sinner's view of God: he regards him as unjust, demanding more than is right." The witnesses note the servant blames his sloth on his lord's supposed harshness — a slander that betrays both his unbelief and his self-justification.

The witnesses note the excuse: the servant excuses his idleness by accusing his lord of being a hard, grasping man whom he feared to serve.

So the witnesses make the verse the slothful servant's slanderous excuse. The lesson the expositors draw is that the unprofitable servant cloaks his sloth in a false fear and a slander of his lord, betraying the sinner's wrong view of God. The servant, the witnesses note, offers as his defence what is in truth his deepest fault: "I feared thee, because thou art an austere man" — he professes a fear of his lord, but it was no right or reverent fear, for true fear of a good master would have moved him to diligence, not idleness. The expositors press the slander hidden in his words: he accuses his lord of being "austere" — hard, grasping, oppressive, one who "takest up that thou layedst not down, and reapest that thou didst not sow," that is, who exacts where he gave nothing and profits by others' toil while living at ease. The witnesses observe that this, as Barnes draws out, is the picture the slothful sinner forms of God: he regards God as a hard taskmaster, unjust and demanding, requiring more than is right — and on this false view excuses his own neglect, as though the fault lay in the severity of his lord rather than his own idleness. The expositors note the self-deception and the unbelief in this: the servant's "fear" was not reverence but a guilty dread that paralyzed rather than spurred him; and his slander of his lord was the false excuse by which he sought to justify his sloth. The witnesses draw the lesson: men who neglect the gifts and grace of God often defend their neglect by a wrong and slanderous view of God — calling him hard, unreasonable, austere — when in truth the fault is wholly their own. The verse thus exposes the heart of the unprofitable servant: a false fear, a slandered lord, and a self-justifying excuse for idleness; and it prepares for the lord's answer, which will turn the servant's own words against him and condemn him out of his own mouth.
22:
"And he saith unto him, Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee, thou wicked servant. Thou knewest that I was an austere man, taking up that I laid not down, and reaping that I did not sow." The witnesses note the lord's condemnation of the servant by his own words. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the argument: "out of thine own mouth — by your own statement... If you knew that this was my character, and that I would be rigid, firm, and even severe, it would have been wisdom in you to have made the best use of the money; but as you knew my character beforehand, you have no right to complain if you are condemned." Barnes adds: "we are not to suppose that God is unjust or austere; but the lesson is that as men know God will be just and call them to a strict account, they ought to be prepared to meet him." The witnesses note the lord does not grant the slander, but argues from the servant's own premise: even were it true, his sloth would be inexcusable.

The witnesses note the lord judges the servant "out of his own mouth" — taking the servant's own words and turning them against him.

So the witnesses make the verse the condemnation of the slothful servant by his own excuse. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sinner's own words and professed knowledge will condemn him, leaving him without excuse before God. The lord, the witnesses note, calls the servant "wicked" — not for any waste, but for his sloth and his slander — and condemns him "out of thine own mouth": by the very excuse the servant offered. The argument, the expositors press (with Barnes), is not that the lord admits the slander — God is not in truth austere or unjust — but that, even granting the servant's own premise, his sloth was inexcusable: "Thou knewest that I was an austere man... " — if you really believed me so rigid and exacting, that very belief should have driven you to the utmost diligence, lest you fall short; your own professed knowledge of my character condemns your idleness, for you acted contrary even to what you claimed to believe. The witnesses observe the searching justice of this: the sinner is judged by his own confession; his excuses become his accusers; what he alleged in defence proves his guilt. The expositors draw the abiding lesson Barnes names: men know that God is just and will call them to a strict account; that very knowledge, far from excusing neglect, condemns it, for it should have moved them to prepare and to be diligent — and they cannot complain if, knowing this, they are condemned for their sloth. The witnesses note the dreadful equity of the day of judgment: God will need no other witness than the sinner's own mouth, his own knowledge, his own excuses, to leave him speechless and condemned. The verse thus turns the servant's slander into his sentence: judged out of his own mouth, the wicked servant stands condemned by the very excuse he hoped would clear him — a warning that the sinner's self-justifications will, at the last, become the ground of his just condemnation.
23:
"Wherefore then gavest not thou my money into the bank, that at my coming I might have required mine own with usury?" The witnesses note the lord's exposure of the servant's inexcusable sloth. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains: "why did you not loan it out, that it might be increased?" — the bank being "the treasury, or the place of exchange." Clarke glosses "usury" as the money's "produce, what the loan is fairly worth, after paying the person sufficiently for using it." Gill draws the point: "had Christ been such a person as he represents him, he ought to have been the more diligent... since he knew that he would, in a rigid manner, demand an account." The witnesses note the lord shows that even the least effort — putting the money to the bankers — would have spared the servant; his utter idleness left him without defence.

The witnesses note the lord names the easiest course the servant might have taken: to lend the pound to the bankers for interest, requiring not even his own labour.

So the witnesses make the verse the final exposure of the servant's inexcusable sloth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the slothful servant could have done at least the least, and his failure even in that condemns him utterly. The lord, the witnesses note, presses the servant's guilt to its sharpest point: "Wherefore then gavest not thou my money into the bank?" — if the servant feared to trade actively, lest he lose by his own want of skill, he might at least have lent the pound to the bankers, who would have paid interest, so that the lord at his coming would have received his own with increase, requiring no risk or labour of the servant himself. The expositors observe the force of the argument: the servant had not even the excuse of incapacity, for the easiest course lay open to him; his sloth was so total that he did not so much as the least; and so, on his own showing, he is condemned. Gill's point, the witnesses press, is that the servant's professed view of his lord ought to have driven him to at least this diligence: knowing (as he claimed) that his lord would rigidly demand an account, he should the more have put the pound to use, even by the simplest means. The witnesses draw the lesson: there is no excuse for the servant who does nothing with the trust of Christ; if he cannot do much, let him do what he can; the least faithful use is accepted, but utter idleness is condemned. The expositors note the searching application: the sinner who neglects the gifts and grace of God cannot plead inability, for some use was always within his reach; his total neglect, when even the least was possible, leaves him speechless. The verse thus completes the lord's exposure of the slothful servant: not only did he fail to trade, he failed even to do the least; and so, condemned out of his own mouth and convicted of inexcusable idleness, he stands ready for the sentence that follows — the loss of the pound he would not use.
24:
"And he said unto them that stood by, Take from him the pound, and give it to him that hath ten pounds." The witnesses note the sentence of forfeiture. The Reformed Gill notes the bystanders "may be the angels," though "not strictly taken, as if any persons will be employed in what follows, only that it will be done." The witnesses note the sentence: the unused pound is taken from the slothful servant and given to the most faithful, who had gained ten — the gift forfeited by neglect transferred to diligence.

The witnesses note the pound is not left with the idle servant nor destroyed, but transferred to the one who had made the best use of his.

So the witnesses make the verse the forfeiture of the neglected trust. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gift unused is taken away, and bestowed on the diligent. The lord, the witnesses note, commands those who stood by — perhaps, as Gill suggests, the angels who execute the King's will, though the figure is not to be pressed — to "take from him the pound": the trust the slothful servant would not use is now removed from him altogether; he loses even what he had. And it is "given to him that hath ten pounds" — transferred to the most faithful servant, who had traded his pound into ten; the gift forfeited by neglect is added to the one who had improved his to the full. The expositors press the principle, which the next verse will state: the unused gift is not allowed to lie idle for ever, but is taken from the unprofitable and given to the diligent, who will use it. The witnesses observe the justice and the wisdom of this: the slothful servant, who hoarded his pound rather than employ it, is judged by losing it; while the faithful, who used his to the utmost, is rewarded with more — for the gifts of God are entrusted to be used, and gravitate, as it were, to those who will improve them. The expositors draw the lesson: to neglect the gifts and grace of Christ is to risk losing them; the talent unused, the opportunity unimproved, the gospel unheeded, may be withdrawn, and given to others more diligent. The witnesses note the solemn truth this teaches against spiritual sloth: the way to keep and increase what God has given is to use it; the way to lose it is to bury it. The verse thus pronounces the forfeiture of the neglected pound and its transfer to the faithful — the just sentence on the servant who would not trade, and the further reward of the servant who did; preparing for the great principle of the kingdom that the next verse will declare.
25:
"(And they said unto him, Lord, he hath ten pounds.)" The witnesses note the bystanders' surprise and a textual point. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads it as "an observation made by some of the by-standers, as if surprised at such a decision: He has already ten pounds — why take away this one and add to what he already possesses?" Clarke notes the verse "is omitted by the Codex Bezae and some copies," and reads it as a remark made by some hearer "with a design to correct" the distribution. The witnesses note the objection: it seems strange that the one who has most should receive yet more, while the one with least loses even that.

The witnesses note the bystanders' protest at the apparent unfairness: giving more to him who already has ten.

So the witnesses make the verse the objection that occasions the great principle. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ways of the kingdom may seem unjust to human reckoning, yet rest on a deeper equity that the next verse will declare. The bystanders, the witnesses note, exclaim, "Lord, he hath ten pounds" — surprised, as Barnes observes, that the pound should be added to the one who already had most, rather than left with the one who had least; to their eyes, it seemed to compound inequality, heaping more on the rich servant and stripping the poor. The expositors note that this protest, whether part of the original text or (as Clarke holds) an early gloss, voices the natural human objection to the kingdom's principle of reward: it appears to give to those who have and take from those who have not. The witnesses observe that the objection is allowed to stand precisely so that Christ may answer it: the next verse will state the principle that resolves the seeming injustice — "unto every one which hath shall be given; but from him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away." The expositors draw the lesson: the rewards and judgments of Christ's kingdom often run contrary to human notions of fairness, for they are measured not by equal distribution but by faithfulness; the diligent, who have improved their trust, are given more, while the slothful, who let theirs lie idle, lose even what they had. The witnesses note that the bystanders' surprise mirrors the murmur of the human heart against grace and judgment alike; but the principle Christ states is just, for the gifts of God are given to be used, and follow faithfulness. The verse thus records the protest that sets up the parable's great maxim, allowing the natural objection to be raised that Christ may answer it with the deeper law of the kingdom — that to him who has shall be given, and from him who has not shall be taken even what he has.
26:
"For I say unto you, That unto every one which hath shall be given; and from him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away from him." The witnesses note the great principle of the kingdom. The grammatical-historical Barnes states it: "these are the words of the nobleman declaring the principles on which he would distribute the rewards of his kingdom... every man who is faithful, and improves what God gives him, shall receive much more." Clarke suggests supplying "gained" — "give it to him who hath gained ten... unto every one who hath gained shall be given; and from him who hath not gained" shall be taken away. The witnesses note the principle resolves the bystanders' objection: the reward follows faithfulness, the diligent gaining more, the slothful losing all.

The witnesses note the law: to the one who has (and uses), more is given; from the one who has not (who lets it lie idle), even that is taken.

So the witnesses make the verse the kingdom's law of increase and forfeiture. The lesson the expositors draw is that faithfulness in the use of God's gifts is rewarded with increase, while neglect ends in total loss. The principle, the witnesses note, answers the bystanders' protest: it is not arbitrary favouritism that gives more to the one with ten, but the just law of the kingdom — "unto every one which hath shall be given." The expositors press the sense Clarke draws out by supplying "gained": it is the one who has and improves, who has gained by faithful use, to whom more is given; faithfulness attracts further trust and reward, the diligent servant being set over more. And "from him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away" — the one who has not used his trust, who has not gained, loses even the gift he received and neglected. The witnesses observe the spiritual law this states, which Christ utters elsewhere (Matthew 13:12; 25:29; Mark 4:25; Luke 8:18): in the things of God, the faithful use of grace increases it, while the neglect of grace forfeits it; gifts unused are gifts withdrawn, while gifts improved are multiplied. The expositors draw the lesson: this is no injustice but the deepest equity, for God's gifts are entrusted to be used, and the use or neglect of them rightly determines their increase or loss; the diligent grow richer in grace, the slothful poorer, until they lose all. The witnesses note the searching application: every hearer of the gospel either improves it and gains more, or neglects it and loses even what he had; there is no standing still. The verse thus declares the abiding law of the kingdom — to him that hath, more; from him that hath not, even that he hath — resolving the objection and pressing upon every servant the urgency of faithful diligence, lest the neglected gift be taken away.
27:
"But those mine enemies, which would not that I should reign over them, bring hither, and slay them before me." The witnesses note the judgment on the rebellious citizens. The Reformed Gill identifies them as "particularly the Jews, who were enemies to the person of Christ, and hated and rejected him, as the King Messiah; and rebelled against him." Ellicott notes "this feature is peculiar to St. Luke's report," with "an historical groundwork in the conduct of Archelaus on his return" (who slew his opponents), and "spiritually, it represents... the ultimate victory of the Christ over the unbelieving and rebellious" (1 Corinthians 15:25): "they who will not have Him to reign over them will learn" his power in judgment. The witnesses note the dreadful end of the enemies: brought before the King and slain in his presence.

The witnesses note the parable closes with the judgment of the rebels, distinct from the servants — those who refused the King's reign destroyed before him.

So the witnesses make the verse the judgment of the King upon his enemies. The lesson the expositors draw is the certain and fearful doom of those who reject the reign of Christ. The parable, the witnesses note, ends not with the servants only but with the citizens — the enemies who had sent their message, "We will not have this man to reign over us" (v.14); and their end is terrible: "bring hither, and slay them before me." Gill reads them, the expositors press, as the Jewish nation, who hated and rejected their Messiah and refused his reign, and on whom fell the fearful judgment of the destruction of Jerusalem; and beyond them, all who reject Christ's rule. Ellicott's note, the witnesses observe, draws both the historical and the spiritual sense: the figure recalls how Archelaus, on receiving his kingdom, destroyed those who had opposed him; and spiritually it sets forth the ultimate victory of Christ over the unbelieving and rebellious — for he must reign till he has put all enemies under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:25), and those who would not have him reign will learn his power in their destruction. The expositors draw the solemn lesson: to refuse the reign of Christ is not a light or safe thing, but open rebellion against the King, which ends in judgment; the rejecters of his grace become the objects of his wrath, slain before the throne they despised. The witnesses note the two issues the parable thus sets side by side: the faithful servants rewarded with rule, the slothful servant stripped, and the rebellious enemies destroyed — the threefold outcome of the King's return, reward and loss and judgment. The verse thus closes the parable of the pounds with the doom of the enemies of Christ's reign, a warning to all who, like the citizens, will not have him to reign over them; for the returning King will reward his faithful, judge his slothful, and destroy his enemies — and none who refuse his crown will escape the reckoning of that day.
28:
"And when he had thus spoken, he went before, ascending up to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the resolute advance to the cross. Titus of Bostra connects it to the parable just spoken: those who saw him going up "thought that He was going then to commence the kingdom of God; when the parable was finished, in which He reproved that error, and showed plainly that He had not yet vanquished that death which was plotting against him, he proceeded forth to His passion, going up to Jerusalem." Bede notes the parable "had been pronounced concerning the end of that city which was about both to slay Him, and to perish itself." The witnesses note Christ "went before" — leading the way, advancing resolutely toward Jerusalem and his passion.

The witnesses note Christ, having corrected the false hope of an immediate kingdom, presses on toward Jerusalem and the cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the resolute approach to Jerusalem. The lesson the expositors draw is the deliberate advance of Christ to his passion, having first corrected the carnal expectations of his followers. Christ, the witnesses note, "having thus spoken" the parable of the pounds — which taught that the kingdom would not appear immediately, but only after his departure and return — now "went before, ascending up to Jerusalem"; he leads the way, the expositors press, with the same steadfast resolve marked earlier (Luke 9:51, "he stedfastly set his face"), toward the city where he must suffer and die. Titus's observation, the witnesses observe, ties the going to the teaching: the parable had reproved the error that the kingdom would commence at once, showing that Christ had not yet conquered the death that awaited him; and so, the false hope corrected, he proceeds to his passion. Bede's note, the expositors observe, adds that the parable bore on the doom of Jerusalem — the city about both to slay him and to perish itself; so his approach is at once to his own death and to the judgment of the city that would reject him. The witnesses draw the lesson: Christ went to Jerusalem not in ignorance nor by compulsion, but knowingly and willingly, having prepared his disciples by his teaching for the events to come; his leading the way is the mark of his voluntary obedience, the Shepherd going before his flock into the valley of his suffering. The expositors note the quiet majesty of the verse: the King who had just spoken of receiving a kingdom now advances to the cross by which he would win it; the ascent to Jerusalem is the ascent to his throne by way of his passion. The verse thus sets Christ on the road to his cross, resolute and leading, having corrected the misunderstandings of his followers; and it prepares for the triumphal entry, in which the King, going up to die, would for a moment be hailed by the multitude as the King he truly was.
29:
"And it came to pass, when he was come nigh to Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount called the mount of Olives, he sent two of his disciples." The witnesses note the setting of the triumphal entry. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the place-names — Bethphage and Bethany, villages on the Mount of Olives near Jerusalem — and weighs whether "of Olives" is the genitive plural (olive-trees) or the place-name Olivet (Acts 1:12). Bede notes "Bethphage was a small village belonging to the priests on Mount Olivet," and "Bethany a little town on the side of the same mountain, about fifteen stades from Jerusalem." The witnesses note Christ, approaching the city, sends two disciples ahead to fetch the colt for his entry.

The witnesses note the deliberate preparation: Christ, near the Mount of Olives, sends two disciples to procure the animal for his royal entry.

So the witnesses make the verse the preparation for the triumphal entry. The lesson the expositors draw is the deliberateness with which Christ arranged his royal entry into Jerusalem, fulfilling prophecy. Christ, the witnesses note, having come near to Bethphage and Bethany on the Mount of Olives — the customary approach to Jerusalem from the east — sends "two of his disciples" ahead, not by accident but by design, to fetch the colt on which he would ride into the city. The expositors press the significance: this entry was no spontaneous or unplanned event, but a deliberate act by which Christ, for once, presented himself openly as the King — fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy that the King would come to Zion "lowly, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass" (Zechariah 9:9). The setting, the witnesses observe, is itself significant: the Mount of Olives, hard by Jerusalem, the place of his agony to come and of his ascension, becomes the starting-point of his royal procession. Bede's and Robertson's notes on the villages and the mount, the expositors observe, ground the narrative in real geography, the eye-witness detail of Luke's account. The witnesses draw the lesson: Christ, who had hitherto avoided public acclaim and forbidden the proclaiming of his Messiahship, now deliberately arranges to enter the royal city as the promised King — his hour being come, and the time to present himself openly to Jerusalem as her Messiah, that her rejection of him might be without excuse. The expositors note the contrast with his usual lowliness: the King who had nowhere to lay his head now enters his city in humble state, on a borrowed colt, yet as the fulfilment of ancient prophecy.
