The verse thus opens the account of the triumphal entry with Christ's deliberate preparation — the sending of the two disciples for the colt — the King setting in motion his royal approach to the city that would crown him with thorns.
30:
"Saying, Go ye into the village over against you; in the which at your entering ye shall find a colt tied, whereon yet never man sat: loose him, and bring him hither." The witnesses note Christ's foreknowledge and the colt's significance. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the meaning of the untrodden colt: "whereon no man ever yet sat — Plummer holds that this fact indicated to the disciples a royal progress into the city, of a piece with the Virgin Birth and the burial in a new tomb." The witnesses note Christ's precise foreknowledge: he tells the disciples exactly what they will find — a colt tied, never ridden — proving his divine knowledge of things at a distance.

The witnesses note the two marks: Christ's exact knowledge of the colt's place, and the colt's fitness as one "whereon yet never man sat."

So the witnesses make the verse the display of Christ's foreknowledge and the fitness of the colt. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the divine omniscience of Christ in directing the disciples, and the royal, sacred fitness of an unridden colt for the King's entry. Christ, the witnesses note, sends the disciples with precise directions: they shall find, at their entering the village, "a colt tied, whereon yet never man sat" — and the exactness of this foreknowledge, the expositors press, is a proof of his deity; he knows, at a distance and beforehand, the very animal, its place, and its untrodden state, as only the omniscient God can know. The witnesses observe the significance of the colt's being one "whereon yet never man sat": Robertson and Plummer note that this marked the entry as a royal progress, for things set apart to sacred or royal use were to be such as had not been put to common use — as the colt was unridden, the tomb new, the virgin's womb untouched. The expositors draw out the fitness: it was meet that the King should ride on an animal never before mounted, dedicated, as it were, to this sacred service; the untrodden colt befits the holiness and the royalty of the One who rides it. The witnesses note the further wonder, drawn out in the verses to come: that this colt, never broken nor ridden, should bear Christ peacefully through a shouting multitude — a miracle in itself, the very beasts owning their Maker. The expositors draw the lesson: every detail of the entry is ordered by Christ's sovereign foreknowledge and fitted to his royal dignity; the King provides for his own procession, knowing all, directing all. The verse thus shows Christ's divine knowledge in the exact directions, and the sacred fitness of the unridden colt for the King's entry — preparing for the disciples' finding all exactly as he said, a confirmation of his word and his deity.
31:
"And if any man ask you, Why do ye loose him? thus shall ye say unto him, Because the Lord hath need of him." The witnesses note Christ's further foreknowledge and the title "the Lord." The Reformed Gill marks the omniscience: "our Lord, being God omniscient, knew this question would be asked by the owners, and therefore prepares his disciples with an answer." Ellicott directs attention to "the meaning of the word 'Lord' as thus used" — the title by which the owners would recognize Christ's claim. The witnesses note Christ foretells the very objection and supplies the answer — "the Lord hath need of him" — which would suffice to release the colt.

The witnesses note Christ's exact foresight of the owners' question, and the authority of the title "the Lord" that would answer it.

So the witnesses make the verse the further proof of Christ's foreknowledge and the assertion of his lordship. The lesson the expositors draw is the omniscience of Christ in foreseeing the objection, and the authority of his claim as "the Lord" over all. Christ, the witnesses note, not only knows where the colt is and its untrodden state, but foresees the very question the owners will ask — "Why do ye loose him?" — and prepares his disciples with the answer beforehand; and Gill marks this as another proof of his deity, the omniscient God knowing the future words of men. The expositors press the significance of the answer Christ supplies: "the Lord hath need of him." The title "the Lord," the witnesses observe (with Ellicott and the moderns), is the very word used both of the owners (as "lords" of the colt, v.33) and of Christ — and Christ here claims it for himself, asserting a lordship that overrides the owner's right: the supreme Lord has need of the colt, and that need is sufficient warrant to release it. The expositors draw the lesson: all things are at the disposal of Christ the Lord, who may claim what he will for his service; the owners' right yields to his, for he is Lord of all. The witnesses note the gentle wonder that the bare word, "the Lord hath need of him," would suffice to make the owners give up their beast — a token both of Christ's authority and of the working of his Spirit in the owners' hearts. The expositors observe that Christ, who possessed all things, yet stooped to "need" a borrowed colt for his royal entry — the Lord of all riding in humble state on another man's beast. The verse thus joins Christ's foreknowledge of the objection to the assertion of his lordship in the answer: "the Lord hath need of him" — a claim that all creation is his to use, and a proof, in its exact fulfilment, of the deity of the King who was riding to his cross.
32:
"And they that were sent went their way, and found even as he had said unto them." The witnesses note the exact fulfilment of Christ's word. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "as he had said unto them — Luke alone notes this item," marking the precise correspondence between Christ's directions and what the disciples found. The witnesses note the disciples' obedience and the confirmation it brought: they went, and found everything exactly as Christ had foretold.

The witnesses note the simple but significant fact: all came to pass precisely as Christ had said, confirming his word.

So the witnesses make the verse the confirmation of Christ's foreknowledge by its exact fulfilment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the precise correspondence of the event to Christ's word proves his divine knowledge and rewards the obedience of his disciples. The disciples, the witnesses note, "went their way" in simple obedience to Christ's command, not questioning the strange directions; and they "found even as he had said unto them" — the colt tied, untrodden, in the place he named; everything exactly as he had foretold. Robertson's note, the expositors observe, marks that Luke alone records this item — "as he had said" — drawing attention to the exact fulfilment; the evangelist would have his readers see that Christ's word was confirmed to the letter. The witnesses press the proof this affords: the precise correspondence between Christ's prediction and the event is a token of his omniscience, for only the God who knows all could so exactly foretell the circumstances at a distance; the fulfilment confirms the deity of the One who spoke. The expositors draw the lesson on obedience: the disciples obeyed without understanding, simply trusting Christ's word, and found it true; so the servant of Christ is to obey his commands even where he cannot see the reason, confident that what Christ has said will be found exactly so. The witnesses note the encouragement: the word of Christ never fails of fulfilment; what he promises or directs comes to pass precisely, and faith that acts on his word is never disappointed. The verse thus records the exact fulfilment of Christ's foreknowledge and the obedience of his disciples — a small detail, yet a confirmation of his deity and a model of trustful obedience; the disciples finding all "even as he had said," as his people in every age find his word made good.
33:
"And as they were loosing the colt, the owners thereof said unto them, Why loose ye the colt?" The witnesses note the owners' question and the wordplay on "lord." The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott draw out the striking use of the same Greek word (kurios) for the "owners" (lords) of the colt and for Christ "the Lord": "the same Greek word for 'owner' and for the 'Lord' affords a striking example of the elasticity of its range of meaning" (Ellicott); the owners "would understand" the title (Robertson). The witnesses note the question came just as Christ had foretold, confirming his word.

The witnesses note the owners' natural question — why are you loosing our colt? — asked exactly as Christ had predicted.

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilment of Christ's foretold objection and the play on lordship. The lesson the expositors draw is the confirmation of Christ's foreknowledge and the contrast between the human "lords" of the colt and the divine Lord who claims it. The owners, the witnesses note, ask precisely the question Christ had foreseen — "Why loose ye the colt?" — the natural protest of men whose beast is being taken; and the exact correspondence to Christ's prediction (v.31) again confirms his omniscience. The expositors press the suggestive wordplay the moderns observe: the "owners" are, in the Greek, the "lords" (kurioi) of the colt — and the answer the disciples are to give names another Lord (kurios), "the Lord hath need of him"; so that the human lords of the beast are confronted with the claim of the divine Lord, before whom their ownership yields. The witnesses observe the elasticity of the word, which can mean an earthly owner or master, or the supreme Lord God; and Christ, the expositors note, deliberately uses the title that the owners would recognize as a claim of authority over their property. The expositors draw the lesson: the lords of the colt must give way to the Lord of all; the property of men is at the disposal of Christ, who, though he condescends to ask and to borrow, has every right to claim what he will for his service. The witnesses note the quiet token of Christ's working: the owners, hearing that the Lord had need of the colt, would release it — their hearts moved to yield to the claim. The verse thus records the owners' question, foretold and fulfilled, and the play on lordship that runs through the account: the human lords of the colt confronted by the divine Lord who has need of it — a small scene that confirms Christ's foreknowledge and quietly asserts his sovereign claim over all that men call their own.
34:
"And they said, The Lord hath need of him." The witnesses note the disciples' answer and its effect. The Reformed Gill notes they "used the words Christ directed them to, and it had the effect he said it would; for the owners both let them go, and the colt with them directly." The witnesses note the simple obedience of the disciples — repeating exactly the words Christ gave them — and the immediate yielding of the owners to the claim of "the Lord."

The witnesses note the bare word, "the Lord hath need of him," sufficed — the owners releasing the colt at once.

So the witnesses make the verse the effectual answer and the owners' yielding. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of Christ's word and the readiness of all things to serve his need. The disciples, the witnesses note, give exactly the answer Christ had supplied — "The Lord hath need of him" — and it has precisely the effect he foretold: the owners let the colt go at once. Gill's observation, the expositors press, marks the double fulfilment: the disciples used the very words Christ directed, and those words had the very effect he promised; both the prediction of the objection and the prediction of its resolution came true. The witnesses observe the wonder in the owners' ready yielding: that men should give up their beast at the bare word that "the Lord hath need of him" — without further question or resistance — is a token both of the authority of the title and of the working of Christ's Spirit in their hearts, inclining them to yield. The expositors draw the lesson: when Christ has need of anything for his service, he can dispose the hearts of men to give it freely; the Lord of all has but to signify his need, and his providence makes the way. The witnesses note the abiding application: Christ still has need of his people's possessions, time, and service for his cause; and the heart truly his will, like these owners, release them gladly at his word, owning that all we have is the Lord's, lent to us, and reclaimable for his use. The expositors observe the gentle majesty of the scene: the King of glory, who owns all things, condescends to "need" a borrowed colt, and the owners, moved by his word, supply it — the Lord served by the willing gift of his creatures. The verse thus records the effectual word and the owners' yielding, confirming once more the truth of Christ's foreknowledge and displaying the readiness with which, at his word, all things are made to serve the need of the Lord who is about to ride into his city.
35:
"And they brought him to Jesus: and they cast their garments upon the colt, and they set Jesus thereon." The witnesses note the disciples' homage in preparing the colt. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains "set Jesus thereon" (a causative verb, "to cause to mount"), and Ellicott and Barnes note the casting of garments on the colt as an act of honour (cf. 2 Kings 9:13, where Jehu was so proclaimed king). The witnesses note the disciples, having no royal trappings, used their own garments as a saddle and a token of homage, and set Jesus upon the colt for his entry.

The witnesses note the spontaneous honour: the disciples spreading their own garments on the colt and setting Jesus thereon, as upon a throne.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' royal homage to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the spontaneous honour rendered to Christ as King, in the humble means at the disciples' disposal. The disciples, the witnesses note, "brought him to Jesus" — the colt obtained, they bring it to their Lord; and then, having no fine housings for a royal mount, they "cast their garments upon the colt," using their own cloaks both to make a seat for Christ and as a token of homage. The expositors press the significance of this act: the casting of garments was, as Barnes notes, a recognized way of honouring a king — when Jehu was proclaimed king, his followers spread their garments under him (2 Kings 9:13); so the disciples, by this act, honoured Jesus as their King, making for him a royal seat of their own clothing. The witnesses observe the spontaneity and the lowliness of the homage: it was not the pomp of an earthly coronation, with rich trappings and a fine steed, but the humble honour of poor men giving what they had — their garments — to dignify the King who rode on a borrowed colt. The expositors draw the lesson: true homage to Christ uses whatever means are at hand, however humble, to honour him; the disciples' cloaks, spread on the colt, were a fitter throne for the lowly King than gold and purple. And "they set Jesus thereon," the witnesses note — placing him upon the colt, as one enthroned, for his entry into the royal city. The expositors observe the fulfilment of prophecy: the King comes to Zion, lowly and riding upon a colt (Zechariah 9:9), enthroned not on a war-horse but on the foal of an ass, his royalty clothed in humility. The verse thus shows the disciples' eager, humble homage — garments for a saddle, the colt for a throne — as they set their King upon the beast for his triumphal yet lowly entry into Jerusalem; the willing honour of his followers to the King who rode to his cross.
36:
"And as he went, they spread their clothes in the way." The witnesses note the multitude's continuing homage along the road. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson draw out the imperfect tense: "they spread" (hypestrōnnyon) describes "the constantly repeated act of casting down the garments as the Lord rode on" (Ellicott) — a continued, repeated spreading as the procession advanced. Ellicott notes Luke, unlike Matthew and Mark, does not mention the branches of trees. The witnesses note the people carpeting the road with their garments — the honour shown to a king on his progress.

The witnesses note the repeated, eager act: the crowd spreading their clothes in the road before Christ as he rode.

So the witnesses make the verse the multitude's royal carpet of homage. The lesson the expositors draw is the spreading honour of the crowd, owning Christ as King as he rode toward the city. The people, the witnesses note, "spread their clothes in the way" — and the imperfect tense, the expositors press (with Ellicott and Robertson), marks a continued, repeated act: as the Lord rode on, the multitude kept casting down their garments before him, carpeting the road with their cloaks, that the King might pass over them. This, the witnesses observe, was the customary honour rendered to a king on his triumphal progress — the spreading of garments in the way, that the royal feet (or here, the colt) might tread upon them, a token of submission and homage. The expositors note the spreading enthusiasm: the homage that began with the disciples' garments on the colt now extends to the whole multitude, who strew the road with their clothes, the acclaim swelling as the procession nears the city. Ellicott's note, the witnesses observe, marks a small distinctive of Luke's account: he records the garments but not the branches of palm and other trees that Matthew, Mark, and John mention — each evangelist supplying his own detail of the scene. The expositors draw the lesson: for this one hour, before his passion, Christ permitted and received the open homage of the people as King — the King of Israel hailed and honoured, his way carpeted with garments, in fulfilment of the prophecy of the coming King; a foretaste of the glory due to him, granted in the very week of his rejection and death. The witnesses note the poignancy: the crowd that spread their garments before him would, within days, cry "Crucify him"; yet for this moment, the King received his due. The verse thus shows the multitude's continuing homage — the road carpeted with their clothes as Christ rode on — the swelling acclaim of the triumphal entry, the King for once openly honoured on his way to the cross.
37:
"And when he was come nigh, even now at the descent of the mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works that they had seen." The witnesses note the joyful praise and its occasion. Origen marks the swelling of the company: "as long as our Lord was in the mount His Apostles only were with Him, but when He began to be near the descent, then there came to Him a multitude." Theophylact reads "disciples" broadly: "not only the twelve or the seventy-two, but all who followed Christ." Bede names the chief cause of their praise: "they marveled most at the resurrection of Lazarus; for as John says, For this cause the people also met him, for that they heard that he had done this miracle" (John 12:18). The witnesses note the multitude praising God aloud for all the mighty works they had seen.

The witnesses note the loud, joyful praise of the whole multitude, breaking forth at the descent of the mount for Christ's mighty works.

So the witnesses make the verse the outburst of joyful praise. The lesson the expositors draw is the fitting response of praise to the mighty works of Christ, rising from the whole multitude of his followers. The crowd, the witnesses note, "began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice" — and the occasion was "all the mighty works that they had seen": the miracles of Christ, his healings, his power over nature and death, which had filled them with wonder and now overflowed in praise. The expositors press the breadth of the company: not the apostles only, but, as Theophylact notes, all who followed Christ — the whole multitude of disciples, swelled (Origen observes) as the procession neared the city — joined in the acclaim. And the chief cause of their wonder, the witnesses observe with Bede, was the recent raising of Lazarus, the crowning miracle that had drawn the people to meet him (John 12:18); the resurrection of one four days dead was the mighty work that most stirred their praise. The expositors draw the lesson: the proper response to the works and grace of Christ is praise to God — loud, joyful, public praise; the multitude could not contain their wonder, but broke forth in acclaim, glorifying God for what they had seen. The witnesses note that this praise, though the crowd's understanding was imperfect, was true and fitting: Christ's mighty works did declare his glory and call for praise, and the people rightly, if unwittingly, hailed their King. The expositors observe the contrast with the silence Christ had often enjoined: now, his hour being come, he permits and accepts the open praise. The verse thus records the joyful outburst of the whole multitude — praising God aloud for Christ's mighty works, above all the raising of Lazarus — the swelling acclaim of the triumphal entry, in which the followers of Christ, for once, gave him the praise his works deserved.
38:
"Saying, Blessed be the King that cometh in the name of the Lord: peace in heaven, and glory in the highest." The witnesses note the content of the acclaim and Luke's distinctive form. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the difference: "the substitution of 'glory' for the 'Hosanna' of St. Matthew and St. Mark is characteristic of the Gentile Evangelist"; and he draws "the parallelism between the shouts of the multitude before the Passion and the song of the angels at the Nativity (Luke 2:14)" — "there the voices spoke of peace on earth; here the multitude, prophesying unconsciously, speak of peace in heaven." Barnes notes the words echo Psalm 118:26 ("Blessed be he that cometh in the name of the Lord") and the angels' "glory in the highest" (Luke 2:14).

The witnesses note the acclaim hails Christ as the King coming in the Lord's name, and ascribes peace and glory to heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse the great acclamation of the triumphal entry. The lesson the expositors draw is the multitude's confession of Christ as the promised King, and the heavenly peace and glory his coming brings. The crowd, the witnesses note, hail Christ in the words of Psalm 118:26 — "Blessed be the King that cometh in the name of the Lord" — owning him as the Messiah, the King of Israel, coming with divine authority and commission; this was the very Psalm sung at the great feasts, and the people apply its blessing to Jesus, confessing him King. The expositors press the distinctive form Luke gives the cry: where Matthew and Mark have "Hosanna," Luke — writing for Gentiles, to whom the Hebrew word would be strange — records "peace in heaven, and glory in the highest"; and Ellicott draws the beautiful parallel with the angels' song at the Nativity (Luke 2:14). The witnesses observe the suggestive contrast: at his birth the angels sang "peace on earth"; now, as he goes to his passion by which that peace would be won, the multitude (prophesying, as Ellicott says, beyond their knowledge) cry "peace in heaven" — for by the cross the breach between heaven and earth would be healed, and peace established between God and man. The expositors draw the lesson: the coming of the King brings peace and glory — peace, reconciliation between heaven and earth, and glory to God in the highest; the acclaim, though the crowd grasped not its full depth, truly named the blessings of Christ's mission. The witnesses note that Luke's framing, joining the entry to the Nativity, sets Christ's whole work between two songs — the angels' at his birth, the multitude's at his passion — both proclaiming peace and glory. The verse thus records the great acclamation — Christ hailed as the King in the Lord's name, with peace in heaven and glory in the highest — the people's true, if imperfect, confession of their King, and a foreshadowing of the peace his cross would win between heaven and earth.
39:
"And some of the Pharisees from among the multitude said unto him, Master, rebuke thy disciples." The witnesses note the Pharisees' demand to silence the praise. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads their motive: they "had all along more or less acknowledged our Lord as a Master (Teacher or Rabbi), and were willing to give Him a fair share of respect as such; to go beyond that, to receive Him as... the king of Israel, the Christ, seemed to them but the wild frenzy of the disciples, which the Master ought to check." Gill notes they had placed themselves in the crowd "to watch and observe what was said and done, that they might have something to reproach Christ with." The witnesses note the Pharisees, offended at the Messianic acclaim, demand that Christ rebuke and silence his disciples.

The witnesses note the Pharisees' offence: willing to call Christ "Master," but scandalized that he should be hailed as King.

So the witnesses make the verse the Pharisees' attempt to silence the praise of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the offence of the self-righteous at the honour given to Christ as King, and their desire to suppress it. The Pharisees, the witnesses note, "from among the multitude" — having mingled in the crowd to watch and find fault — were scandalized by the acclaim, and demanded, "Master, rebuke thy disciples." The expositors press the revealing form of their address, which Ellicott draws out: they call Christ "Master" (Teacher, Rabbi) — the title they were willing to grant him, a measure of respect as a teacher; but they could not bear that he should be hailed as "the King that cometh in the name of the Lord," the Messiah, the King of Israel — this seemed to them blasphemous extravagance, the frenzy of misguided disciples, which the Teacher himself ought to check. The witnesses observe the heart of their offence: they would allow Christ a place among the rabbis, but not the throne of the Messiah; they would honour him as a man, but not own him as King. The expositors draw the lesson: this is the perennial offence of the self-righteous and the unbelieving — to grant Christ some respect as a great teacher, a good man, a moral guide, but to reject the higher claim, to be scandalized at his being worshipped as King and Lord. The witnesses note the malice in the demand: the Pharisees, watching for occasion against Christ, sought to make him silence the very praise that was his due, hoping either to suppress his Messianic claim or to find ground of accusation. The expositors observe that Christ's answer (the next verse) will utterly refuse their demand, declaring that the praise could not be silenced. The verse thus records the Pharisees' attempt to quench the acclaim of Christ as King — willing to call him Master but not King — the offence of those who would grant him anything but his crown; and it prepares for Christ's resolute vindication of his disciples' praise.
40:
"And he answered and said unto them, I tell you that, if these should hold their peace, the stones would immediately cry out." The witnesses note Christ's refusal and the force of his saying. The Reformed Gill notes Christ spoke "with great earnestness, fervour, and courage." Barnes reads it as proverbial: "it is proper that they should celebrate my coming; their acclamations ought not to be suppressed... So joyful is the event — the coming of the Messiah — that it is not fit that I should attempt to impose silence on them"; the language being "strong proverbial language" for the impossibility and impropriety of silencing the praise. The witnesses note Christ's striking declaration: if the disciples were silenced, the very stones would cry out.

The witnesses note Christ refuses to rebuke the praise, declaring that it could not and should not be silenced — the stones themselves would proclaim him.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's vindication of his disciples' praise. The lesson the expositors draw is the fitness and irrepressibility of the praise of Christ, which not even the silencing of his disciples could suppress. Christ, the witnesses note, answers the Pharisees not by checking his disciples, as they demanded, but by defending their praise with great courage: "if these should hold their peace, the stones would immediately cry out." The expositors press the force of the saying. It declares, first, the fitness of the praise: so great is the event — the coming of the Messiah, the King — that it must be celebrated; it would be wrong to suppress so just an acclaim, for the glory of the occasion demands it. And it declares, second, the irrepressibility of the praise: were the disciples silenced, creation itself — the very stones — would break out in proclamation, rather than that the coming of the King should pass unhonoured. The witnesses observe that the language is, as Barnes notes, proverbial and hyperbolic, expressing strongly the truth that the praise of Christ cannot finally be silenced; yet it carries a real weight — that if men refuse him praise, the creation he made will not be silent, and the dumb things will witness to their Maker and King. The expositors draw the lesson: the glory of Christ will be proclaimed, whether by his willing people or, failing them, by the witness of creation; those who would silence his praise fight against a thing that cannot be stopped. The witnesses note the rebuke to the Pharisees: in demanding silence, they set themselves against the very acclaim that heaven and earth owed the King; and Christ's refusal exposes their offence. The verse thus records Christ's bold vindication of his disciples' praise — that the coming of the King must be celebrated, and that, were his people silent, the stones would cry out — a declaration of the fitness and the irrepressibility of the worship due to him, and a refusal to let the Pharisees quench the honour of his hour.
41:
"And when he was come near, he beheld the city, and wept over it." The witnesses note Christ's tears over Jerusalem. Origen sees in it the Saviour's own beatitude fulfilled: "because He had said, Blessed are they that weep, He Himself also wept over the city." Cyril reads it as the mark of his compassion and his true humanity: "Christ had compassion upon the Jews, who wills that all men should be saved; which had not been plain to us were it not revealed by a certain mark of His humanity, for tears poured forth are the tokens of sorrow." Gregory the Great applies it to the elect who fall away: "our Redeemer does not cease to weep through His elect whenever he perceives any to have departed from a good life to follow evil ways." The witnesses note the contrast: amid the joyful acclaim, the King weeps over the city that would reject him.

The witnesses note the striking scene: at the height of his triumph, beholding Jerusalem, Christ weeps.

So the witnesses make the verse the weeping of Christ over Jerusalem. The lesson the expositors draw is the compassion of the Saviour over a doomed and impenitent people, even in the hour of his own triumph. As Christ came near and "beheld the city" — Jerusalem, the holy city, spread before him from the descent of Olivet — he "wept over it"; and the witnesses note the poignancy of the moment: surrounded by the joyful acclaim of the multitude, the King himself is moved to tears, not by his own coming sufferings, but by the impending ruin of the city that would crucify him. The expositors press the revelation of Christ's heart that the tears afford: Cyril marks that the very weeping is a token of his true humanity and his real compassion — for tears are the sign of sorrow, and Christ's tears show that he genuinely grieved over the Jews, willing that all should be saved, and mourning the destruction they were bringing on themselves. Origen's note, the witnesses observe, sets it as the fulfilment of his own beatitude — he who blessed those that weep himself wept; the Saviour practised the mercy he taught. Gregory's application, the expositors note, extends it: Christ still weeps, as it were, through his elect, over all who depart from grace to ruin. The witnesses draw the lesson: the tears of Christ over Jerusalem are the proof that the sinner's destruction is no light thing, but a grief even to the Saviour; that he takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked, but weeps over their impenitence; and that the doom of the lost is real and dreadful, for Christ would not weep over an imagined danger. The expositors observe the wonder of divine love: the King wept over the city that would kill him, grieving more for her sin and ruin than for his own cross. The verse thus unveils the compassionate heart of Christ in his tears over Jerusalem — a Saviour weeping over the lost — and prepares for his lament over the city's blindness and the prophecy of her destruction that follow.
42:
"Saying, If thou hadst known, even thou, at least in this thy day, the things which belong unto thy peace! but now they are hid from thine eyes." The witnesses note the broken lament and the wordplay on Jerusalem's name. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the emotion: "the emphatic repetition of the pronoun... speaks of the strongest possible emotion; the broken form of the sentence, If thou hadst known..., with no corresponding clause." Adam Clarke draws out the play on the name: "our Lord here alludes to the meaning of the word Jerusalem (he shall see peace); because the inhabitants had not seen this peace and salvation... therefore he said, Now they are hidden from thine eyes." The witnesses note the lament over Jerusalem's lost opportunity — the day of grace, the things of peace, now hidden from her blinded eyes.

The witnesses note the broken, emotional lament: had Jerusalem but known, in her day, the things of her peace — but now they are hidden.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's lament over Jerusalem's blindness. The lesson the expositors draw is the tragedy of a day of grace despised, and a salvation hidden from those who would not see it. Christ's words, the witnesses note, are broken with emotion — "If thou hadst known, even thou..." — the sentence left incomplete, the strongest token of grief, as Ellicott observes; the heart too full to finish. The lament is over Jerusalem's blindness to "the things which belong unto thy peace" — the salvation, the reconciliation with God, the deliverance that Christ offered, which she had failed to recognize. The expositors press the wordplay Clarke draws out: Jerusalem means "he shall see peace," yet she had not seen the peace held out to her in her Messiah; the city of peace knew not the things of her peace. And "now they are hid from thine eyes" — the witnesses observe the solemn turn: the day of opportunity, "this thy day," had been despised, and now the things of peace were hidden, a judicial blindness following her refusal; the grace once offered and rejected was now withdrawn from her sight. The expositors draw the lesson: there is a day of grace, an accepted time, in which the things of peace are offered; but if that day is despised, the opportunity may pass, and the salvation once within reach be hidden from the eyes that would not see it. The witnesses note the warning to every soul and city: to neglect the day of visitation, to refuse the things of peace while they are offered, is to risk their being hidden for ever; grace despised may be grace withdrawn. The expositors observe the grief in the "but now" — the door once open now closing, the peace once offered now hidden. The verse thus records Christ's broken lament over Jerusalem's blindness — her day of grace despised, the things of her peace now hidden — a warning that the rejected gospel may be withdrawn, and the season of mercy, once past, return no more.
43:
"For the days shall come upon thee, that thine enemies shall cast a trench about thee, and compass thee round, and keep thee in on every side." The witnesses note the precise prophecy of the siege. Adam Clarke marks its literal fulfilment: "this was literally fulfilled when this city was besieged by Titus. Josephus gives a very particular account of the building of this wall, which he says was effected in three days, though it was thirty-nine furlongs in circumference; and that, when this wall and trench were completed, the Jews were so enclosed that no person could escape, and no provision brought in, so that they were reduced to terrible distress by famine." Ellicott notes the Greek for "trench" means "the stockade or palisade" of a besieging army's earthwork. The witnesses note Christ foretells the siege of Jerusalem in exact detail — the encircling rampart that would shut the city in.

The witnesses note the detailed prediction: a trench cast about the city, encircling it, shutting it in on every side.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophecy of Jerusalem's siege. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty and exactness of the judgment Christ foretold, fulfilled to the letter in the destruction of the city. Christ, the witnesses note, turns from lament to prophecy: "the days shall come upon thee" — the days of judgment, soon to fall (within forty years) upon the city that despised her day of grace. And the prophecy is precise: "thine enemies shall cast a trench about thee, and compass thee round, and keep thee in on every side" — the encircling siege-works, the rampart and palisade, that would shut the city in. The expositors press the literal fulfilment Clarke records: when Titus besieged Jerusalem in AD 70, he built, as Josephus relates, a wall of circumvallation around the city — thirty-nine furlongs in circuit, completed in three days — that enclosed the inhabitants so completely that none could escape and no provision enter, reducing them to the horrors of famine. The witnesses observe the exactness: Christ foretold not the bare fact of destruction, but the very manner of it — the trench, the encircling, the shutting-in — and history confirmed every detail. The expositors draw the lesson: the word of Christ, whether in grace or in judgment, is sure and exact; what he foretells comes to pass to the letter, and his prophecies are confirmed by their fulfilment. The witnesses note the solemnity of the judgment foretold: the city that would not know the things of her peace would know instead the horrors of the siege — the just recompense of rejected grace. The expositors observe that Christ wept even as he foretold this doom, his tears mingling with his prophecy; the judge who pronounced the sentence grieved over the condemned. The verse thus records Christ's exact prophecy of the siege of Jerusalem — the encircling trench, the shutting-in — fulfilled precisely by Titus in AD 70, a proof of the certainty of his word and the dreadfulness of the judgment that follows despised grace.
44:
"And shall lay thee even with the ground, and thy children within thee; and they shall not leave in thee one stone upon another; because thou knewest not the time of thy visitation." The witnesses note the completeness of the destruction and its cause. Lightfoot sets Christ's verdict against the rabbinic guesses: "the Masters dispute the reason of the laying-waste of Jerusalem" — one says for profaning the sabbath, another for neglect of the phylacteries, others for various sins — "but Wisdom saith, Jerusalem was destroyed because she knew not the time of her visitation." Lightfoot notes "all those great good things promised to mankind were to happen in the last days" — the days of the Messiah; and Jerusalem, failing to know that her visitation had come, was destroyed. The witnesses note the total ruin — not one stone left on another — and its root cause: she "knewest not the time of thy visitation."

The witnesses note the cause of the doom: Jerusalem failed to know the time of her visitation — the coming of her Messiah.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophecy of total destruction and its assigned cause. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ruin of Jerusalem sprang from her failure to know the time of her visitation, the coming of her Messiah. Christ foretells, the witnesses note, the utter destruction: the city laid even with the ground, her children slain within her, and "not one stone upon another" — a completeness fulfilled in the razing of Jerusalem and the temple by the Romans. And he names the cause: "because thou knewest not the time of thy visitation." The expositors press the meaning: God had visited Jerusalem in the person of her Messiah — the long-promised King had come to her, offering the things of her peace; this was her day of visitation, the accepted time, the supreme moment of opportunity. But she "knew it not" — failed to recognize her Messiah, rejected the grace held out, and so brought upon herself the judgment of despised mercy. Lightfoot's striking note, the witnesses observe, sets this divine verdict against the rabbinic speculations: the Jewish masters debated whether Jerusalem fell for sabbath-breaking, or neglect of the law, or various sins; but the true reason, "Wisdom saith," was that "she knew not the time of her visitation" — the rejection of the Messiah was the crowning sin that sealed her doom. The expositors draw the lesson: the gravest of sins is the failure to know and receive Christ when he visits; the day of visitation, despised, brings the heaviest judgment. The witnesses note the warning to every soul and people: God visits in grace, offering peace; to fail to know the time of visitation, to reject the visiting Christ, is to invite the ruin that befell Jerusalem. The verse thus closes the lament with the prophecy of total destruction and its cause — the city razed because she knew not her visitation — a solemn warning that the despised day of grace ends in irreparable judgment.
45:
"And he went into the temple, and began to cast out them that sold therein, and them that bought." The witnesses note the cleansing of the temple. Gregory the Great draws the connection to the foregoing: "when He had related the evils that were to come upon the city, He straightway entered the temple, that He might cast out them that bought and sold, showing that the destruction of the people arose chiefly from the guilt of the priests." Ambrose presses the lesson: "God wishes not His temple to be a house of traffic, but the dwelling-place of holiness; nor does He fix the priestly service in a salable performance of religion, but in a free and willing obedience." The witnesses note Christ, entering the temple as King and Lord, drives out the buyers and sellers who had profaned it.

The witnesses note Christ's first act on entering the temple: to cleanse it of the traffic that defiled it.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing of the temple. The lesson the expositors draw is Christ's zeal for the holiness of God's house and his judgment on those who profane it. Christ, the witnesses note, having wept over the city and foretold its doom, "went into the temple" — and his first act, as King and Lord come to his Father's house, was to "cast out them that sold therein, and them that bought," driving out the merchants and money-changers who had turned the court of God's house into a market. Gregory's observation, the expositors press, ties the cleansing to the lament: the destruction of the people sprang chiefly from the guilt of the priests, who had allowed and profited from this profanation; so Christ, having foretold the city's ruin, enters the temple to expose the corruption at its heart. Ambrose's note, the witnesses observe, draws the principle: God will not have his temple a house of traffic, but a dwelling-place of holiness; nor will he have religion made a matter of merchandise, a salable performance, but a free and willing obedience — and the buyers and sellers had made the worship of God a trade. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ is jealous for the holiness of his Father's house, and will not suffer it to be profaned by greed and traffic; he comes as Lord to his temple (as Malachi foretold, 3:1), to purge and cleanse it. The witnesses note the authority of the act: the King, having entered his city in triumph, now exercises his lordship over the temple, cleansing it as his own. The expositors observe the spiritual application often drawn: as Christ cleansed the material temple, so he would cleanse the temple of the heart, driving out the defilements of worldliness and greed that profane the soul made for God's dwelling. The verse thus records Christ's cleansing of the temple — his zeal for its holiness and his judgment on its profaners — the Lord come to his house to purge it, and a warning against making the worship of God a matter of gain.
46:
"Saying unto them, It is written, My house is the house of prayer: but ye have made it a den of thieves." The witnesses note the scriptural ground of the cleansing. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Wesley note Christ cites Isaiah 56:7 ("My house shall be called a house of prayer") and Jeremiah 7:11 ("Is this house... become a den of robbers?"). The witnesses note the contrast Christ draws: God's house was appointed for prayer, but the traffickers had made it a den of thieves — a place of robbery and extortion under the cloak of religion.

The witnesses note Christ's twofold charge from Scripture: the temple was meant to be a house of prayer; they had made it a den of thieves.

So the witnesses make the verse the scriptural justification of the cleansing. The lesson the expositors draw is the true purpose of God's house and the guilt of those who pervert it to gain. Christ, the witnesses note, grounds his act in the written word: "It is written, My house is the house of prayer" — citing Isaiah's declaration that God's house should be a house of prayer for all nations (Isaiah 56:7); the temple was appointed for the worship of God, the offering of prayer and praise, the meeting of the soul with its Maker. But, Christ charges, "ye have made it a den of thieves" — echoing Jeremiah's rebuke (Jeremiah 7:11); the traffickers and money-changers, with their extortion and greed, had turned the place of prayer into a place of robbery, a den where thieves shelter, profaning the holy court with covetous gain. The expositors press the contrast: nothing could be further from the purpose of God's house than to make it a market and a den of extortion; the very place set apart for communion with God had been desecrated by the love of money. The witnesses observe the authority of the appeal to Scripture: Christ does not act by arbitrary zeal but by the written word, which both declares the temple's true purpose and condemns its profanation. The expositors draw the lesson: the worship of God is not to be mingled with worldly gain, nor his house made a place of merchandise; the spirit of the trafficker — using religion for profit — is an abomination in the house of prayer. The witnesses note the abiding warning to the Church: that the house of God, and the things of God, must be kept holy, devoted to prayer and worship, not turned to covetous ends. The verse thus gives the scriptural ground of Christ's cleansing — the house of prayer made a den of thieves — declaring the true purpose of God's house and the guilt of profaning it for gain.
47:
"And he taught daily in the temple. But the chief priests and the scribes and the chief of the people sought to destroy him." The witnesses note Christ's daily teaching and the leaders' murderous plot. Bede names the causes of their enmity: "either because He daily taught in the temple, or because He had cast the thieves therefrom, or that, coming thereto as King and Lord, He was greeted with the honour of a heavenly hymn of praise." Cyril notes the irony: "from what Christ had said and done it was meet that men should worship Him as God, but far from doing this, they sought to slay Him." The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies "the chief of the people" as "the first of the people," the notables of Jerusalem. The witnesses note the contrast: Christ teaching daily in the temple, while the leaders plotted his death.

The witnesses note the double scene: Christ openly teaching day by day, the rulers secretly seeking to destroy him.

So the witnesses make the verse the contrast between Christ's open teaching and the rulers' deadly enmity. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of Christ's ministry and the hardened malice of the leaders against him. Christ, the witnesses note, "taught daily in the temple" — having cleansed it, he now used it for its true purpose, filling it with the word of God; for the days before his passion he taught openly, fearlessly, in the very temple, dispensing the gifts of grace to all who would hear. But against this gracious ministry, the expositors press, the leaders set their malice: "the chief priests and the scribes and the chief of the people sought to destroy him." Bede names the causes of their enmity, the witnesses observe: his daily teaching, which drew the people and exposed their error; his cleansing of the temple, which struck at their gain and their authority; and the royal acclaim he had received, which they could not bear. Cyril's note, the expositors observe, marks the dreadful irony: Christ's words and works proved him worthy of worship as God, yet the leaders, far from worshipping, sought to slay him — their hardened hearts turning the very evidence of his deity into fresh occasion for hatred. The witnesses draw the lesson: the clearer the light of Christ, the fiercer the opposition of those resolved to reject him; his grace and truth, which should have won them, only hardened their enmity. The expositors note the courage of Christ: knowing the leaders sought his life, he yet taught daily and openly in the temple, not shrinking from his work nor hiding from his enemies, but pursuing his ministry to the end. The verse thus sets Christ's gracious daily teaching against the rulers' murderous plot — the Saviour dispensing the word while his enemies sought his death — preparing for the passion now drawing near; and it displays both the persistence of his love and the depth of the malice that would crucify him.
48:
"And could not find what they might do: for all the people were very attentive to hear him." The witnesses note the rulers' frustration and the people's eagerness. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the vivid phrase: "very attentive — literally, hung upon him to hear him; the word denotes an anxious desire, a fixed attention, a cleaving to him, an unwillingness to leave the place where the word of God is preached." Barnes adds the application: "this is always the case when men become anxious about their salvation." The witnesses note the rulers, though resolved on Christ's death, "could not find what they might do" — restrained by the people's devotion to him, which gave them no opportunity.

The witnesses note the people hung upon Christ's words, and this very devotion frustrated the rulers' murderous designs.

So the witnesses make the verse the providential restraint of Christ's enemies by the people's attention. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rulers' malice was held in check by the people's eager devotion to Christ, until his hour should come. The chief priests and scribes, the witnesses note, sought to destroy him, "and could not find what they might do" — they were resolved on his death, but could find no opportunity, no way to seize him, restrained by the press of the people who clung to him; their malice was real but, for the moment, impotent. The reason, the expositors press, was the people's devotion: "for all the people were very attentive to hear him." Barnes's vivid rendering, the witnesses observe, captures it — the people "hung upon him to hear him," with an anxious, fixed attention, cleaving to him, unwilling to leave the place where he taught; so eager were they for his word that the rulers dared not lay hands on him publicly, lest the people resist. The expositors note the providence in this: Christ's hour was not yet come, and God restrained his enemies by the very devotion of the multitude, holding back their malice until the appointed time of the passion; the people's eagerness to hear was, unknown to them, the means of protecting Christ for those last days of teaching. The witnesses draw the application Barnes names: this hanging upon the word, this fixed and anxious attention, is the mark of souls awakened to their need of salvation; men so concerned for their souls cleave to the preaching of the gospel, unwilling to miss a word. The expositors observe the contrast at the chapter's close: the rulers plotting death, the people hanging on his words, and Christ teaching on undisturbed — the malice restrained, the ministry continuing, until the hour should strike. The verse thus ends the chapter with the rulers frustrated and the people devoted: Christ's enemies held back by providence, his word eagerly received, his teaching pursued to the end — the calm before the storm of the passion, in which the love of the people and the hand of God together preserved the Saviour for his appointed hour.
